verses.text: In those days was Hezekiah sick unto death. And the prophet Isaiah the son of Amoz came to him, and said unto him, Thus saith the LORD, Set thine house in order; for thou shalt die, and not live. Then he turned his face to the wall, and prayed unto the LORD, saying, I beseech thee, O LORD, remember now how I have walked before thee in truth and with a perfect heart, and have done that which is good in thy sight. And Hezekiah wept sore. Guidance: The death sentence and the king's tearful prayer. 'In those days' Hezekiah falls 'sick unto death,' and Isaiah brings the hardest possible word: 'Set thine house in order; for thou shalt die, and not live.' This is a divine sentence delivered through the prophet — the king is to make his final arrangements. Hezekiah's response is striking: he does not rage or despair; he 'turned his face to the wall, and prayed unto the LORD.' He turns from the messenger to the One who sent the message, taking his case directly to God. His prayer appeals to his record: 'remember now how I have walked before thee in truth and with a perfect heart.' (This is not self-righteous boasting; 'perfect heart' means whole-hearted, undivided devotion — the very trait for which he was praised in 18:3-6. He appeals to the integrity of his covenant relationship with God.) 'And Hezekiah wept sore' — he wept bitterly. The chapter is showing us a believer's honest response to a death sentence: he prays, he appeals to God's covenant faithfulness, and he weeps. There is no pretense of stoic acceptance; there is raw, tearful petition. The chapter is teaching us that even a divine 'thou shalt die' may be brought before God in prayer — Hezekiah does not treat the prophet's word as the absolutely final, unappealable end, but takes it as an occasion to seek the LORD's face. The chapter is teaching us the legitimacy of pouring out our grief and pleading our case before God in the face of death and disaster. (Why Hezekiah desired more years is debated — partly the natural human clinging to life, partly that he had as yet no son to continue the Davidic line; Manasseh, born in the added years, would be three years away.) The chapter is teaching us that God invites such honest, tearful prayer — and, as the next verses will show, sometimes answers it with astonishing mercy.

And it came to pass, afore Isaiah was gone out into the middle court, that the word of the LORD came to him, saying, Turn again, and tell Hezekiah the captain of my people, Thus saith the LORD, the God of David thy father, I have heard thy prayer, I have seen thy tears: behold, I will heal thee: on the third day thou shalt go up unto the house of the LORD. And I will add unto thy days fifteen years; and I will deliver thee and this city out of the hand of the king of Assyria; and I will defend this city for mine own sake, and for my servant David's sake. Guidance: The LORD relents — fifteen years added. The answer comes with astonishing speed: 'afore Isaiah was gone out into the middle court' — before the prophet had even left the palace — the word of the LORD reverses the sentence. 'Tell Hezekiah... I have heard thy prayer, I have seen thy tears: behold, I will heal thee: on the third day thou shalt go up unto the house of the LORD.' The healing is so complete that within three days Hezekiah will be well enough to walk up to the temple. 'And I will add unto thy days fifteen years' — a specific, generous extension of life. And the LORD throws in deliverance: 'I will deliver thee and this city out of the hand of the king of Assyria... for mine own sake, and for my servant David's sake.' (This dates the illness to around the time of the Assyrian crisis; the same grounds — God's own sake and David's — that defended the city in 19:34 sustain the king here.) The chapter is showing us the LORD's tender responsiveness to honest prayer and tears. 'I have heard thy prayer, I have seen thy tears' — God notices both the words and the weeping. The same God who had said 'thou shalt die' now says 'I will heal thee,' because the king turned to Him in prayer. The chapter is teaching us a profound truth about prayer and the divine will: the LORD genuinely responds to the prayers of His people; the prophetic sentence was real, but it was also an invitation to seek God, and seeking Him brought mercy. The chapter is teaching us that God is moved by the tears of His children — He keeps account of them (Ps 56:8) — and that earnest, faith-filled prayer can be the means through which He grants His mercy. The chapter is also a caution we will see unfold: the added fifteen years were a mercy, but they would bring complications (the Babylonian envoys of v12, and the birth of the wicked Manasseh) — a reminder that even answered prayers for what we desire come with consequences only God fully foresees.

And Isaiah said, Take a lump of figs. And they took and laid it on the boil, and he recovered. Guidance: The fig poultice — means and miracle together. Isaiah prescribes a practical remedy: 'Take a lump of figs,' which is laid on the boil (the inflamed sore of Hezekiah's illness), and 'he recovered.' Fig poultices were a known folk remedy for such inflammations in the ancient world. The chapter is showing us a beautiful pairing of the supernatural and the ordinary. The healing was unmistakably a miracle — the LORD had announced it, reversed a death sentence, and would confirm it with the sundial sign — yet it came THROUGH an ordinary medical means, a fig poultice. The chapter is teaching us that God's miraculous working and the use of ordinary means are not opposed. The LORD who could have healed Hezekiah by a bare word chose to involve a physical remedy, just as Elisha used salt for the water and meal for the poison. The chapter is teaching us not to set faith and means against each other: it is not unbelief to take the medicine, nor is it mere medicine when God has promised the healing. The LORD often gives His gifts through ordinary channels, and the wise believer both trusts the LORD and uses the means He provides. The chapter is teaching us that the fig poultice did not heal Hezekiah — the LORD did — but the LORD was pleased to heal through it, uniting His promise and His provision in a single act of mercy.

And Hezekiah said unto Isaiah, What shall be the sign that the LORD will heal me, and that I shall go up into the house of the LORD the third day? And Isaiah said, This sign shalt thou have of the LORD, that the LORD will do the thing that he hath spoken: shall the shadow go forward ten degrees, or go back ten degrees? And Hezekiah answered, It is a light thing for the shadow to go down ten degrees: nay, but let the shadow return backward ten degrees. And Isaiah the prophet cried unto the LORD: and he brought the shadow ten degrees backward, by which it had gone down in the dial of Ahaz. Guidance: The sign of the reversed shadow. Hezekiah asks for a confirming sign, and Isaiah offers a choice: shall the shadow on the sundial (the 'dial of Ahaz,' a stairway or sundial built by his father) go forward ten degrees, or backward ten degrees? Hezekiah reasons shrewdly: 'It is a light thing for the shadow to go down ten degrees' — a shadow naturally advances forward, so that would prove little; 'nay, but let the shadow return backward ten degrees' — let it do the impossible, move BACKWARD, against the sun's course. Isaiah cries to the LORD, and 'he brought the shadow ten degrees backward.' The sun's shadow reversed — a sign in the heavens themselves. The chapter is showing us the LORD's willingness to confirm His word with a sign suited to the believer's request, and a sign of staggering cosmic scope — the One who 'made heaven and earth' (19:15) commands even the movement of light and shadow. The chapter is teaching us about the LORD's power over the created order: the same God who could turn back an army (ch 19) can turn back a shadow; nothing in the physical universe is beyond His control. The chapter is teaching us that God sometimes graciously gives signs to strengthen faith (as He did for Gideon, for Moses, for Ahaz — though Ahaz refused his) — not because faith should demand signs, but because the LORD condescends to our weakness. The chapter is also showing us Hezekiah's discernment in choosing the harder sign — he wanted a confirmation that could only be God's doing, not something explainable by nature. The reversed shadow on the very dial his apostate father Ahaz had built becomes a quiet emblem: where Ahaz refused a sign and trusted Assyria, the faithful son receives a sign and trusts the LORD, and even the works of the faithless father are made to serve the LORD's confirmation of His word.

At that time Berodachbaladan, the son of Baladan, king of Babylon, sent letters and a present unto Hezekiah: for he had heard that Hezekiah had been sick. And Hezekiah hearkened unto them, and shewed them all the house of his precious things, the silver, and the gold, and the spices, and the precious ointment, and all the house of his armour, and all that was found in his treasures: there was nothing in his house, nor in all his dominion, that Hezekiah shewed them not. Guidance: The Babylonian envoys and Hezekiah's vanity. A delegation arrives from Merodach-baladan (Berodach-baladan) of Babylon — at this time a rising power and an enemy of Assyria, seeking allies. The pretext is to congratulate Hezekiah on his recovery; the real purpose was surely to feel out Judah as a potential ally against Assyria. And here the great king of faith stumbles. 'Hezekiah hearkened unto them, and shewed them all the house of his precious things' — silver, gold, spices, fine oil, his whole armoury, all his treasures: 'there was nothing in his house, nor in all his dominion, that Hezekiah shewed them not.' He gave the Babylonians a complete inventory of Judah's wealth and military resources. (2 Chr 32:25-26, 31 explains the heart-issue: after his healing 'Hezekiah rendered not again according to the benefit done unto him; for his heart was lifted up' — and God 'left him, to try him, that he might know all that was in his heart.') The chapter is showing us the sad vulnerability of even a great man of faith to pride. The king who trusted the LORD against Assyria's whole army now preens before flattering foreign envoys, showing off his treasures — pride and the desire for impressive alliances getting the better of his discernment. The chapter is teaching us that the dangers of prosperity and flattery can succeed where the dangers of threat and terror failed: Hezekiah stood firm under the Rabshakeh's intimidation but faltered under Babylon's flattery. The chapter is teaching us that pride often follows hard on the heels of God's greatest mercies — Hezekiah had just been healed and delivered, and instead of humble gratitude his 'heart was lifted up.' The chapter is teaching us to be most watchful precisely after great blessings, when the temptation to take credit and seek the world's admiration is strongest. And it is teaching us the folly of trusting in and displaying our resources to the world: the very treasures Hezekiah proudly displayed to Babylon would one day be carried OFF to Babylon (next verses) — what we boast in to the world, the world will eventually take.

Then came Isaiah the prophet unto king Hezekiah, and said unto him, What said these men? and from whence came they unto thee? And Hezekiah said, They are come from a far country, even from Babylon. And he said, What have they seen in thine house? And Hezekiah answered, All the things that are in mine house have they seen: there is nothing among my treasures that I have not shewed them. And Isaiah said unto Hezekiah, Hear the word of the LORD. Behold, the days come, that all that is in thine house, and that which thy fathers have laid up in store unto this day, shall be carried into Babylon: nothing shall be left, saith the LORD. And of thy sons that shall issue from thee, which thou shalt beget, shall they take away; and they shall be eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon. Guidance: Isaiah's prophecy of the Babylonian exile. Isaiah comes and probes with two questions: 'What said these men? and from whence came they?' and 'What have they seen in thine house?' Hezekiah's answers reveal the full extent of his folly: they came from Babylon (a far country), and 'there is nothing among my treasures that I have not shewed them.' Then Isaiah delivers the devastating prophecy: 'Behold, the days come, that all that is in thine house... shall be carried into Babylon: nothing shall be left.' Everything Hezekiah proudly displayed will be looted by the very nation he displayed it to. And worse: 'of thy sons... shall they take away; and they shall be eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon.' His own descendants will be carried to Babylon as castrated court-servants (a fate fulfilled in Daniel and his companions, Dan 1). The chapter is showing us the first explicit prophecy in Kings of the Babylonian exile — the doom that the whole book has been building toward and that will close it (ch 25). Long before Babylon was the great threat (Assyria still loomed), Isaiah names Babylon as the future destroyer of Judah. The chapter is teaching us that pride and worldly alliance-seeking sow seeds of future judgment: Hezekiah's vain display to Babylon becomes the occasion for the announcement that Babylon will one day take it all. The chapter is teaching us the sobering principle that our sins, even the sins of a good man, can have consequences that ripple out to later generations — the exile Isaiah foretells would fall not on Hezekiah but on his descendants over a century later. The chapter is teaching us to hold our treasures and our security loosely, and to beware the pride that parades them before the world; what we glory in before men is precisely what is most vulnerable, and the LORD's word stands certain over the long future: 'nothing shall be left, saith the LORD.'

Then came Isaiah the prophet unto king Hezekiah, and said unto him, What said these men? and from whence came they unto thee? And Hezekiah said, They are come from a far country, even from Babylon. And he said, What have they seen in thine house? And Hezekiah answered, All the things that are in mine house have they seen: there is nothing among my treasures that I have not shewed them. And Isaiah said unto Hezekiah, Hear the word of the LORD. Behold, the days come, that all that is in thine house, and that which thy fathers have laid up in store unto this day, shall be carried into Babylon: nothing shall be left, saith the LORD. And of thy sons that shall issue from thee, which thou shalt beget, shall they take away; and they shall be eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon. Guidance: Isaiah's prophecy of the Babylonian exile. Isaiah comes and probes with two questions: 'What said these men? and from whence came they?' and 'What have they seen in thine house?' Hezekiah's answers reveal the full extent of his folly: they came from Babylon (a far country), and 'there is nothing among my treasures that I have not shewed them.' Then Isaiah delivers the devastating prophecy: 'Behold, the days come, that all that is in thine house... shall be carried into Babylon: nothing shall be left.' Everything Hezekiah proudly displayed will be looted by the very nation he displayed it to. And worse: 'of thy sons... shall they take away; and they shall be eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon.' His own descendants will be carried to Babylon as castrated court-servants (a fate fulfilled in Daniel and his companions, Dan 1). The chapter is showing us the first explicit prophecy in Kings of the Babylonian exile — the doom that the whole book has been building toward and that will close it (ch 25). Long before Babylon was the great threat (Assyria still loomed), Isaiah names Babylon as the future destroyer of Judah. The chapter is teaching us that pride and worldly alliance-seeking sow seeds of future judgment: Hezekiah's vain display to Babylon becomes the occasion for the announcement that Babylon will one day take it all. The chapter is teaching us the sobering principle that our sins, even the sins of a good man, can have consequences that ripple out to later generations — the exile Isaiah foretells would fall not on Hezekiah but on his descendants over a century later. The chapter is teaching us to hold our treasures and our security loosely, and to beware the pride that parades them before the world; what we glory in before men is precisely what is most vulnerable, and the LORD's word stands certain over the long future: 'nothing shall be left, saith the LORD.'

Then said Hezekiah unto Isaiah, Good is the word of the LORD which thou hast spoken. And he said, Is it not good, if peace and truth be in my days? Guidance: Hezekiah's response — submission, or self-interest? Hezekiah's reply to the prophecy of his descendants' exile is double-edged: 'Good is the word of the LORD which thou hast spoken.' On its face this is humble submission to God's word — accepting the justice of the LORD's verdict without protest, like Eli's 'It is the LORD: let him do what seemeth him good' (1 Sam 3:18). But the second sentence reveals a more troubling undercurrent: 'Is it not good, if peace and truth be in my days?' Hezekiah finds the prophecy bearable largely because the disaster will not fall in HIS lifetime — there will be peace and security in his own days, and the exile is someone else's problem. The chapter is showing us a genuinely mixed response. The submission to God's word is real and commendable; Hezekiah does not rage or bargain. But the relief that 'it won't happen to me' exposes a self-focused complacency — a willingness to accept a future catastrophe for his children and nation as long as his own days are peaceful. The chapter is teaching us to examine the quality of our acceptance of God's word: it is one thing to submit to God's will out of trust and humility; it is another to be at peace with coming judgment merely because we ourselves will escape it. The chapter is teaching us the danger of a short-sighted, self-centered contentment that says, in effect, 'as long as I am comfortable, the troubles of the next generation do not deeply move me.' A truly godly heart would have grieved over the coming exile of his children and, like the great intercessors, pleaded for mercy on those who would come after. The chapter holds both truths in tension: Hezekiah submits (good), but his submission is colored by a relief in his own immunity that falls short of the deepest love. The chapter is teaching us to care about the spiritual inheritance we leave to those who come after us, not merely to be content that the storm will break after we are gone.

And the rest of the acts of Hezekiah, and all his might, and how he made a pool, and a conduit, and brought water into the city, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? And Hezekiah slept with his fathers: and Manasseh his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: Hezekiah's close — and the tunnel that still stands. The reign of Judah's great king of faith ends with the standard formula, with one notable engineering achievement singled out: 'how he made a pool, and a conduit, and brought water into the city.' This is the famous Siloam Tunnel — a 1,750-foot channel Hezekiah cut through solid rock to bring the waters of the Gihon spring inside Jerusalem's walls, securing the city's water supply against the Assyrian siege (the tunnel and its commemorative inscription survive to this day, one of the great archaeological confirmations of the biblical narrative). The chapter is closing the account of a king of remarkable faith and accomplishment — the reformer who trusted the LORD, the man whose prayer turned back an army and added fifteen years to his life, the engineer who secured his city. And the succession: 'Manasseh his son reigned in his stead.' The chapter is teaching us that even the greatest reigns end, and that the LORD's work continues through the line of David despite the gathering shadows. But the name of the successor is ominous: Manasseh, born in the added fifteen years, will be the worst king Judah ever had (ch 21) — a sobering footnote to the mercy of v6. The chapter is teaching us, in this quiet transition, that the godliest father may be followed by the most wicked son, and that the answered prayer for longer life brought into the world the very king who would lead Judah toward the exile Isaiah had just foretold. The reign of faith ends; the long slide toward Babylon resumes. The chapter is teaching us to be grateful for the great works of faith the LORD accomplishes through His servants, while remaining sober about the fragility of every human legacy: Hezekiah's tunnel still flows, but his throne passed to a son who would undo nearly all his spiritual reforms.
